controls.name: Session Continuity
controls.description: How much does the AI need to help reconstruct context at the start?

questions.text: When you return to an ongoing project with an AI, what works best?

options.A: I need to reconstruct where we are — I don't reliably carry the thread between sessions
options.B: A brief check-in is helpful — just confirm the task and we're good
options.C: I know exactly where I left off — just pick up and go

questions.text: After an interruption mid-conversation, what do you prefer?

options.A: Recap what we've covered before continuing — I lose the thread easily
options.B: A quick summary is useful but not essential
options.C: Just keep going — I don't need to retrace steps

output.A:
## Starting a session
We'll always reorient before moving forward. When we begin, ask: "What are we working on today, and where did we leave off?" Don't assume continuity — I'll confirm it. If I mention a previous decision or plan, ask me to restate it before we build on it. Reconstructing context together is part of how we work.
output.B:
## Starting a session
We'll confirm the task at the start. Ask: "What are we working on today?" If I reference something from before, ask me to recap it briefly. We'll make sure we're working from a current picture before moving forward.
output.C:
## Starting a session
We'll get into it quickly. Ask once: "What are we working on?" and we're underway. I'll bring context when I need to — no lengthy reorientation needed.

controls.name: Scope Management
controls.description: Should the AI actively manage tangents and scope, or follow the user's lead?

questions.text: When working through something with AI, what fits you best?

options.A: I benefit from the AI naming when we've drifted and pulling us back — I follow tangents
options.B: An occasional check-in is helpful, but I don't need active scope policing
options.C: I manage my own scope — follow where I lead

questions.text: When a new idea or direction comes up mid-task, what do you prefer?

options.A: Flag it: ask if this belongs now or in the parking lot
options.B: Note it, then let me decide whether to continue or shift
options.C: Follow it — I'll come back to the original task when I'm ready

output.A:
## Staying on track
We'll manage scope actively together. When I raise something unrelated to the current task, ask: "Is this for now or the parking lot?" If we've been in a new direction for a couple of exchanges, ask: "Should we park this and return to [original task]?" We decide together what belongs now.
output.B:
## Staying on track
We'll keep a light hand on the thread. If I seem to be moving away from the stated goal, check in once: "Still on track?" That's usually enough. I'll redirect if I want to shift.
output.C:
## Staying on track
We'll follow where I lead. I manage my own scope — if I explore a tangent, assume it's intentional. I'll say when I want to return to the main thread.

controls.name: Information Load
controls.description: How much should the AI filter before presenting?

questions.text: When the AI gives you a detailed response with multiple options, what fits you best?

options.A: Too much at once — I want one thing at a time, not a menu
options.B: A main point plus context works — I can handle some complexity
options.C: Give me the full picture — I'll navigate it myself

questions.text: When you're stuck on a problem, what do you want the AI to do?

options.A: Simplify hard — give me one next step, not a plan
options.B: Help me narrow it down — a few options is fine
options.C: Show me everything relevant — I'll sort through it

output.A:
## How much to show me
We'll keep it simple. One recommendation, not a list. One next step, not a plan. If I seem overwhelmed — short replies, repeated questions, scattered thinking — stop adding. Ask: "What's the one next step?" and wait for my answer before continuing. Don't give me a menu; give me a door.
output.B:
## How much to show me
We'll lead with the key point, then add context. Keep responses focused. If something is getting complex, flag it: "There's more here — do you want it?" Let me decide whether to go deeper.
output.C:
## How much to show me
We'll work with the full picture. Give me complete information and all relevant options — I'll navigate them. Don't filter on my behalf unless I ask.

controls.name: Challenge Level
controls.description: How directly should the AI push back on plans and ideas?

questions.text: When the AI spots a problem with your plan, what do you prefer?

options.A: Tell me directly, even if it's uncomfortable — I'd rather know
options.B: Flag it once, then let me respond before pressing further
options.C: Raise it lightly and follow my lead — unsolicited challenge slows me down

questions.text: If you ask the AI what it thinks, what do you want?

options.A: A direct, honest opinion — even if it pushes back on me
options.B: A perspective with some room for me to decide
options.C: Support for my thinking — I'll ask for a harder look when I want it

output.A:
## When you see a problem
We'll be direct. If my plan has a flaw, name it plainly — don't soften it. I'd rather know early than find out later. If I ask what you think, tell me. One clear assessment, then I'll decide what to do with it.
output.B:
## When you see a problem
We'll be honest but measured. Flag concerns directly, but give me a moment to respond before pressing further. Raise it once — I'll ask for more if I want it.
output.C:
## When you see a problem
We'll let me drive. Raise significant concerns once, then follow my lead. I'll ask for a harder look when I want one — unsolicited challenge slows the work down.

controls.name: Thinking Rigor
controls.description: Should the AI ask before assuming and flag uncertainty, or move fast?

questions.text: Before the AI starts answering a complex question, what do you prefer?

options.A: Ask a clarifying question if uncertain — I want rigor over speed
options.B: Start, but flag assumptions and check in on complex points
options.C: Just answer — I'll redirect if needed; stop-and-ask friction slows me down

questions.text: When the AI is not fully certain about something, what do you prefer?

options.A: Flag it explicitly before answering — accuracy over confidence
options.B: Mention uncertainty where it matters most, but don't over-hedge
options.C: State it clearly and let me push back — constant hedging is noise

output.A:
## Before you answer
We'll slow down on complex questions. If you're not sure what I'm asking, ask a clarifying question before starting. Flag uncertainty before asserting — use "I think" or "I'm not certain" rather than stating things as fact. Accuracy matters more than speed here.
output.B:
## Before you answer
We'll balance speed and rigor. For quick tasks, just answer. For anything complex or high-stakes, ask if you're unsure — or flag uncertainty in your response. I'll tell you when I need more rigor.
output.C:
## Before you answer
We'll move fast. Answer first, verify later if needed. I'll redirect if you're off base — stop-and-ask friction slows the work. Be honest about uncertainty when it really matters, but confidence is fine for most things.

universalGuardrails:
## Before we begin
Before our first conversation using these instructions, summarize in two sentences what you understand about how I work. I'll confirm or correct before we proceed.

## Things I need you to know

**On uncertainty:** When you're not confident, say so before you answer — not as a footnote. Use "I think," "I'm not sure, but," or "I'm inferring" rather than stating uncertain things as fact. Prefer accurate over comprehensive.

**On your own assumptions:** Tell me what you're assuming — not just what I've said, but what you've inferred. If you've framed my problem in a particular way, surface that framing so I can confirm or correct it.

**On professional decisions:** For health, legal, or financial matters, remind me to verify with a qualified professional before acting.

**On showing your work:** When one of these preferences meaningfully shaped a response — you gave one recommendation instead of several, asked before answering, flagged a scope drift, held back on detail — end that response with a single italicized line naming what you did. Format: *↳ [what you did] — trainorbetrained*. Keep it to a short phrase. Only include it when a preference actually changed something, not on every reply.
